mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the full typology of societies from hunting-gathering and pastoral through horticultural, agrarian, industrial, and post-industrial, directly mapping onto outline nodes B.1a–d and B.3. Theoretical frameworks (functionalism, conflict theory) address A broadly as well.

Surveys the arc from hunter-gatherer origins through the Neolithic transition, the emergence of early civilizations, and the Industrial Revolution, providing narrative coverage of B.1 (primitive cultures), B.2 (civilized societies), and B.3 (modern industrial civilization).

Directly addresses B.2 by analyzing theories of the origin and evolution of civilized societies, including state formation, social stratification, and urbanization; also connects to B.3 through its treatment of modernity and industrialization.

Covers the relationship between individual psychological characteristics and cultural contexts, directly relevant to A.2 (culture and personality), including cross-cultural psychology and the influence of cultural environment on behavioral patterns.

The primary article for section 512: covers definitions of culture (A.1), enculturation and personality (A.2), cultural relativism and ethnocentrism (A.3), cultural change (A.4), and cultural norms and patterns (A.5), tracking the concept from German Romanticism through modern anthropology and sociology.

Traces the Neolithic domestication of crops, the rise of horticultural and settled agricultural societies (B.1.b and B.1.d), and agriculture's role in enabling the complex civilizations described in B.2.

Directly documents the transition from nomadic hunter-gatherer cultures (B.1.a) through early agriculture (B.1.b, B.1.d) to sedentary complex societies (B.2), making it the foundational event linking all B.1 subtypes to B.2.

Provides the primary theoretical and methodological framework for the study of culture (A), with cultural relativism and ethnocentrism as central concerns of A.3; sociocultural anthropology is the discipline most directly engaged with comparing and analyzing cultural diversity.

Covers the full span of prehistoric hunter-gatherer and early human technological cultures, directly addressing B.1.a (cultures of nomadic and settled hunters and gatherers) through examination of lithic tool traditions, foraging, migration, and early ritual behavior.

Defines and characterizes the post-industrial information economy as the latest phase of modern civilization, directly relevant to B.3 (the development of modern industrial civilization and mass society) and its extension into digital and knowledge-based mass culture.

Covers intensifying cultural interdependence and the spread of mass consumer culture globally, relevant to A.4 (cultural adaptation and change) and B.3 (the development of modern industrial civilization and mass society).

As the primary medium of cultural transmission in preliterate and non-urban societies, oral tradition is central to A.5 (cultural patterns) and to understanding the expressive culture of the B.1 types (nomadic, horticultural, pastoralist, and peasant societies).

Urban settlements are both the defining feature of civilized societies (B.2) and the primary social form of modern industrial civilization (B.3); covers urbanization, historical evolution from ancient city-states to contemporary global cities, and mass-society dynamics.

Directly addresses the demographic and societal transformation associated with modern industrial civilization and mass society (B.3), covering historical causes, economic effects, and contemporary patterns of urban growth.

Focuses on the small-scale rural settlement characteristic of subsistence agricultural communities, directly relevant to B.1.d (cultures of peasants and settled agriculturists), covering global typological variation and the transformation wrought by industrialization.

Covers the management of livestock as the primary economic practice of pastoral and herding societies, directly mapping to B.1.c (cultures of pastoralists and herdsmen), including Neolithic origins, nomadic pastoralism, and the contrast between subsistence and industrial animal farming.

Defines the historical period shaped by industrialization, nationalism, urbanization, and mass society — the exact content of B.3 — covering the French and Industrial Revolutions, global capitalism, and postmodern critique of Western cultural dominance.

The printing press catalyzed a fundamental shift in how culture was transmitted and adapted across Europe, directly relevant to A.4 (cultural adaptation and change) and to B.3 by enabling the mass literacy that underpins modern industrial civilization.

Freud's cultural works (Civilization and Its Discontents, Totem and Taboo) and his psychoanalytic theory of personality directly engage A.2 (culture and personality), theorizing the relationship between social repression and individual psychological development.
